extracted_information: The web content provides a comprehensive tutorial on observability in distributed systems, covering its definition, benefits, distinction from monitoring, challenges in distributed environments, and detailed explanations of the three pillars of observability: traces, metrics, and logs. It showcases hands-on implementation using industry standards and popular tools like OpenTracing (with Jaeger), OpenCensus (with Prometheus and Grafana), and Elastic Stack (Elasticsearch, Logstash, Kibana, Beats). The tutorial also touches upon the future of observability, highlighting OpenTelemetry as a unified standard.

specifications.observability_definition: The ability to measure the internal state of a system only by its external outputs.

telemetry_data_types.description: Lines of text an application generates at discrete points during code execution, often structured and with severity levels. Easy to generate but can carry performance costs and require tools for collection, storage, and analysis.
Values represented as counts or measures, calculated or aggregated over a time period, expressing data about a system (e.g., memory consumption). Can come from host, application, or cloud platform.

opencensus_terminology.Measure: A metric type to be recorded (e.g., size of request payload). Identified by name, description, and unit.
opencensus_terminology.Measurement: A data point produced after recording a quantity by measure (e.g., 80 kb for a request payload size measure).
opencensus_terminology.View: Coupling of an aggregation applied to a measure, optionally with tags. Supports aggregation methods like count, distribution, sum, last value. Composed of name, description, measure, tag keys, and aggregation.
opencensus_terminology.Tags: Key-value pairs associated with recorded measurements for contextual information, distinguishing, and grouping metrics. Can be propagated as request headers.

telemetry_data_types.description: Representation of distributed events as a request flows through a distributed system. Helpful for locating bottlenecks, defects, or other issues in distributed systems.

opentracing_terminology.Span: Individual unit of work done in a distributed system. A trace is a directed acyclic graph (DAG) of spans. Spans contain references to other spans, recreating the life of a request.
opentracing_terminology.References: Edges between spans, defining relationships. Two types: 'ChildOf' and 'FollowsFrom'.
Span_State_Capture: The start time-stamp and the finish time-stamp
A set of key-value span tags (for querying and filtering trace data)
A set of key-value span logs (for capturing logging messages or debugging output)
opentracing_terminology.SpanContext: Ties spans together, carrying data across process boundaries. Comprised of implementation-dependent state (like spanId, traceId) and baggage items (key-value pairs crossing process boundary).

observability_vs_monitoring.monitoring: Watching a system's state through a predefined set of metrics and logs, primarily for known failures. Typically deals with low cardinality data.
observability_vs_monitoring.observability: Helps understand the internal state of a system, allowing arbitrary questions about its behavior. Aids in building knowledge about dynamic system behavior, often requiring high cardinality data.
observability_vs_monitoring.cardinality: Refers to the number of unique items in a set. High cardinality data is needed for arbitrary questions about system behavior.

specifications.distributed_system_complexity: Involves many moving components, dynamic scalability with uncertain instance numbers, making collection, curation, and storage of telemetry data difficult. Requires all components (applications, network, load balancer, databases, messaging platforms) to be observable. Pinpointing anomaly sources is challenging due to interdependencies.

features.name: Benefits of Observability
details: Invaluable for investigating situations where a system deviates from its intended state.
Useful to prevent system failures by allowing remedial actions based on alerts from observable data.
Provides important analytical insights to tune the system for better experience.
Helps handle unanticipated problems in complex, dynamically changing distributed systems (especially across public/private cloud and on-premise environments).

features.name: OpenTracing (Jaeger)
details: Vendor-neutral APIs and instrumentation for distributed tracing.
Jaeger UI for querying, filtering, and analyzing trace data with visualization.
Ability to visualize causal relationships between spans (time-axis or graph).

features.name: OpenCensus (Prometheus, Grafana)
details: Libraries for collecting metrics and distributed traces (used for metrics in the example).
Vendor-neutral metrics collection through exporters.
Prometheus: Open-source monitoring solution, scrapes metrics endpoints.
Grafana: Visualization tool integrating with Prometheus for elegant and efficient metric visualization; supports custom dashboards and pre-built ones.

features.name: Elastic Stack (Elasticsearch, Logstash, Kibana, Beats)
details: Log management platform for collecting, storing, and analyzing logs from multiple distributed components.
Elasticsearch: Distributed, RESTful, JSON-based search engine for storing log documents (flexibility, scalability).
Logstash: Log aggregator for collecting, transforming, enhancing, and shipping data.
Beats: Lightweight data shippers (agents) for efficient data collection.
Kibana: Visualization layer on top of Elasticsearch for intuitive log query, filter, and visualization.
Ability to create a production-grade log data pipeline, extensible with buffers like Kafka.

features.name: OpenTelemetry
details: Unified abstraction for both traces and metrics (merger of OpenTracing and OpenCensus).
Future plan to support logs for a complete observability framework.
Support for several languages, integrates with popular frameworks and libraries.
Backward compatible with OpenTracing and OpenCensus via software bridges.

temporal_info.historical_context: Observability roots in control theory; gained popularity as social networking sites scaled (e.g., Twitter). Microservices architecture and cloud computing made debugging difficult, leading to Application Performance Management (APM) tools (AppDynamics, Dynatrace). These tools were initially metrics-based. OpenTracing and OpenCensus projects emerged, now merging into OpenTelemetry.

references: Prometheus (open-source monitoring, graduated project under CNCF)
Elastic Stack (Elasticsearch, Logstash, Kibana, Beats by Elastic)
Kafka (mentioned as a buffer for log data pipeline)
Logz.io (combines ELK, Prometheus, Jaeger as PaaS)
